content:
AUDIENCE:
The reader is a busy senior business executive with no technical or scientific background. They are familiar with broad public health issues only at a high level, such as pandemics or drug resistance, but lack detailed knowledge of microbiology or medical science. Their main concerns are understanding the business and societal impacts of issues, such as risks to workforce health, supply chains, and costs. They prefer concise, clear explanations without jargon or complex scientific detail. Overly technical or detailed explanations will bore or confuse them. They value practical relevance, urgency, and straightforward language. A respectful, authoritative, and clear tone that connects the issue to real-world consequences will resonate best.

===BEGIN===
Antibiotic resistance is spreading because bacteria are adapting to the medicines designed to kill them. This happens when antibiotics are overused or used incorrectly—in humans, animals, and agriculture. For example, taking antibiotics for viral infections or not finishing a prescribed course gives bacteria a chance to survive and become stronger. Resistant bacteria then multiply and can spread between people and places. This trend is worrying because it makes infections harder and more expensive to treat, threatening global health and economies. Without effective antibiotics, common medical procedures and treatments could become risky. To slow this, we need smarter use of antibiotics, better infection control, and investment in new treatments. Understanding this helps businesses prepare for future health challenges that can impact workforce productivity and costs.
===END===
